story:
I found the man in the dirty overcoat hunched in a corner of a grimy bar, nursing a beer and a cigarette. He denied being the Ferryman, but his pallor gave him away—Skippers grew ghostly from time drag, their features thinning toward translucence. I slid him another drink and said I wanted a destination. Not a memorial tour—no Titanic, no Ardennes, no permitted sites covered by the Temporal Protection Act. Off-road.

He laughed and warned me that legal jumps only go to disasters history has insulated, and even those require teams of jumpers to hold the door open. Time resists being handled, he said; the more significant the moment, the more crushing the weight. I told him I didn’t want revenge or money. I just wanted to see my wife again. I was still wearing the suit from her funeral.

That sobered him. “Call me Dennis,” he said. He wouldn’t take me back, but he could open a window—a gray-area view into a moment. No stepping through. He named a price that made me swallow hard. I paid.

Out back, under red neon, Dennis rolled up his sleeves and felt along the air like a blind man searching for a latch. He found a weak spot and peeled reality back like the corner of a rug, revealing a rippling plane of static. “Hold my hand. Think of the where and when.” I pressed my palm to the cool surface—it felt like a TV screen—and the picture stabilized: sunbaked pavement, an intersection, my wife Nicole paused mid-step. Sound and smell poured through. “I can hold it for a couple minutes,” Dennis said, bracing himself against the flap of air he’d pried back.

There she was: laughing, phone to her ear, telling me she loved me as the walk signal blinked. Halfway across, the grocery bag split. A soup can rolled. She lunged. Screeching tires. I’d heard it through the phone before. Seeing it in crisp focus was a punch I couldn’t take.

I picked up a chunk of concrete.

Dennis shouted for me to stop. I swung. The “screen” spiderwebbed. The barrier blew outward in a glittering blast, hurling us into a dumpster. The static roared, pulling at everything like a storm drain. Dennis, bleeding, scrambled to close the tear. I panicked, thinking only of reaching Nicole. In a terrible, desperate moment I drove a jagged shard into his back to stop him. It was wrong. He sagged against the dumpster’s rim and, through blood-flecked breath, told me what I’d done: I’d created an anomaly that would fix itself by erasing me—pinning me to the moment until it balanced. “You can’t hurt time if you never existed.”

The pull tore me in. Static, then heat. I hit pavement. Nicole waited at the curb. The light changed. The bag split. The can rolled. I lunged. Tires screamed. The car hit me, not her.

Static. Heat. Pavement. The scene reset. I rose with sudden, terrible clarity. I could still save her. I only had to take her place—every time. The can rolled. I dove. Tires, impact, darkness.

Again: sun, signal, laughter, split, roll. And each time, the same choice. Not a victory, not a loophole, but the punishment I’d earned: an endless loop where she lives and I die, over and over, so her last breath is never the one I hear.
